Investment Approach

  • Fidelity® Short-Term Bond Fund (FSHBX) is a diversified, short-term bond strategy that seeks a high level of current income, consistent with preservation of capital.
  • Benchmarked against the Bloomberg U.S. 1-3 Year Government/Credit Bond Index, the fund seeks to deliver competitive risk-adjusted performance that is commensurate with investor expectations of a high-quality short-term bond fund.
  • Utilizing a team-based investment process, the fund relies on experienced portfolio managers, research analysts and traders. We concentrate on areas where we believe we can repeatedly add value, including asset allocation, sector and security selection, yield-curve positioning and opportunistic trading.
  • Robust governance and risk management, consisting of extensive quantitative modeling, formal and informal portfolio reviews, and proprietary tools, support the identification of both opportunities and risks.

Bond funds entail interest rate risk (as interest rates rise bond prices usually fall), the risk of issuer default, issuer credit risk and inflation risk. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks. Leverage can increase market exposure and magnify investment risk.

Taxable Bond Market Review

U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds advanced 1.10% in the fourth quarter of 2025, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. This result capped a year of elevated volatility, yet one in which the bond market posted gains in all four quarters. For the full year, the Aggregate index rose 7.30%, its best annual result since 2020.

Quarterly performance was fueled by a combination of elevated starting yields and price appreciation, as bond prices rose amid falling interest rates and investor demand, supported by stable fundamentals. The U.S. Federal Reserve, after resuming the monetary easing program it had paused from January until September of this year, followed through with additional policy rate reductions in October and December. The three rate cuts in 2025's second half, each of a quarter percentage point (25 basis points), brought the federal funds rate down to a target range of 3.5% to 3.75%, from a peak range of 5.25% to 5.5%, which the central bank maintained from July 2023 to September 2024.

The reductions drove short-term bond yields lower and contributed to gains for the investment-grade bond market in October and November (both +0.62%). Following its December meeting, though, Fed officials signaled they might hold steady for now. With progress on inflation stalled – monthly metrics continued to show U.S. consumer inflation moving within a tight range around 3%, above the Fed's 2% target – and labor-market statistics softening but remaining relatively resilient, Fed Chair Jerome Powell indicated a wait-and-see stance and said, "We're well-positioned to see how the economy evolves from here."

This announcement shifted the market's expectation for additional rate cuts deeper into 2026, and the Aggregate index took a small step back in December (-0.15%).

For the three months, short-term bond yields fell in step with the benchmark interest rate, but long-term yields rose marginally, a function of the Fed's policy signals as well as heavy issuance of long- term Treasurys to fund U.S. government spending.

Due to this steepening of the yield curve, short- and intermediate- term issues fared best in Q4. Bonds with maturities of 7 to 10 years posted the strongest advance at 1.52%, while 1- to 3-year bonds rose 1.18%, 3- to 5-year maturities were up 1.32%, and 5- to 7-year bonds climbed 1.36%. Conversely, long-term securities of 10+ years were weakest (-0.00%). By credit rating, higher-quality bonds rated AAA and AA outperformed investment-grade debt rated A and BAA (the Bloomberg equivalent to BBB).

At the sector level, U.S. Treasurys (+0.90%) slightly outperformed investment-grade corporate bonds (+0.84%), which were constrained by historically tight credit spreads. (Notably, for the full year, corporates advanced 7.77%, comfortably besting the 6.38% gain for Treasurys). Other yield-advantaged, credit-sensitive sectors – including agency mortgage-backed securities (+1.71%), commercial mortgage-backed securities (+1.34%) and asset-backed securities (+1.25%) – exhibited relative strength in Q4, while government-related bonds (+1.12%) finished roughly in line with the broader market.

Outside the Aggregate index, returns varied widely. U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities gained just 0.13%, per Bloomberg. But investor interest in higher-yielding assets drove strong results for higher-income categories. U.S. high-yield corporate bonds (+1.31%) outperformed investment-grade corporates, while high-yield emerging-markets debt (4.85%) delivered the bond market's best return. For the year, investment-grade and high-yield emerging- markets bonds led all categories, gaining 9.45% and 13.93%, respectively, aided by improving fundamentals and a weaker U.S. dollar.■

Performance Review

DETAILED FUND ATTRIBUTION RELATIVE TO BENCHMARK

Strategy: Sector Allocation

Market Environment

Fixed-income assets advanced modestly in Q4. Most yield-advantaged sectors outperformed U.S. Treasurys, particularly in the short-duration part of the curve.

Fund Positioning

(Impact vs. Benchmark)

Overall, sector positioning contributed to the fund's performance versus the benchmark Bloomberg U.S. 1-3 Year Government/ Credit Bond Index.

  1. Overweighting U.S. corporate bonds aided relative performance. (Positive)
  2. Non-benchmark allocations to asset-backed securities, mortgage-backed securities and commercial mortgage-backed securities each also helped. (Positive)
  3. In contrast, an underweight stake in government-related bonds detracted from the fund's relative result. (Negative)

Strategy: Security Selection

Market Environment

By term, intermediate bonds with maturities of 7 to 10 years had the strongest gain. By credit rating, higher-quality bonds rated AAA and AA outperformed lower-quality investment-grade debt.

Fund Positioning (Impact vs. Benchmark)

Security selection among investment-grade corporate bonds added relative value the past three months.

  • Within the industrial sector, picks among consumer cyclical and consumer non-cyclical corporates contributed. (Positive)

Strategy: Duration and Yield Curve

Market Environment

The U.S. Treasury yield curve steepened in Q4, with bond yields falling at the front end of the curve and rising at the long end.

Fund Positioning

(Impact vs. Benchmark)

The fund's yield-curve positioning detracted.

  • The fund was underweight one-year maturities in favor of two- and three-year positions. (Negative)
  • The fund's overall duration was generally in line with that of the benchmark. (Neutral)

Outlook and Positioning

At the end of 2025, the global and U.S. business cycles remained constructive, with U.S. monetary and fiscal easing likely to continue in 2026. U.S. trends included strong corporate fundamentals and earnings momentum, softening labor markets, and to date a resilient consumer sector.

Nominal 10-year U.S. Treasury yields ended Q4 effectively unchanged from the previous quarter at roughly 4.2%, after beginning the year around 4.6%. The Fed eased monetary policy in the second half of 2025, cutting its short-term policy rate by 75 basis points and ending quantitative tightening (after reducing its balance sheet by $2.5 trillion since 2022).

The market currently anticipates two more Fed rate reductions in 2026, based on expectations for weakening employment trends and near-term inflation, though those cuts may not come until mid-year or later. The yield curve steepened in 2025 amid concerns over fiscal deficits, persistent inflation and Fed independence, all of which pressured long-term yields.

As of December 31, about 36% of the fund's assets were invested in corporate bonds. Within corporates, financials remained our largest sector allocation, at roughly 22% of total assets. The portfolio also owned about 42% U.S. Treasurys and a 16% non-benchmark allocation to asset-backed securities, including securitized car loan debt and collateralized loan obligations. Consistent with our strategy, the fund was notably underweight Treasurys, which represented approximately 72% of the benchmark at year-end.

In a dynamic environment, we continue to find pockets of value within the fixed-income market, based on our view of pricing and fundamentals. Our goal remains to work with our investment teams to try to find attractively priced bonds for the portfolio while maintaining a disciplined approach to risk management.

It is important to reiterate that the portfolio is constructed with a careful emphasis on security selection, especially with consideration to liquidity and financial resiliency. Investing is a long-term endeavor, and we're focused on generating strong risk-adjusted performance over a full market cycle.■

CHARACTERISTICS

Duration is a measure of a security's price sensitivity to changes in interest rates. Duration differs from maturity in that it considers a security's interest payments in addition to the amount of time until the security reaches maturity, and also takes into account certain maturity shortening features (e.g., demand features, interest rate resets, and call options) when applicable. Securities with longer durations generally tend to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than securities with shorter durations. A fund with a longer average duration generally can be expected to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than a fund with a shorter average duration.

30-day SEC Yield is a standard yield calculation developed by the Securities and Exchange Commission for bond funds. The yield is calculated by dividing the net investment income per share earned during the 30-day period by the maximum offering price per share on the last day of the period. The yield figure reflects the dividends and interest earned during the 30-day period, after the deduction of the fund's expenses. It is sometimes referred to as "SEC 30-Day Yield" or "standardized yield".

data.title: Single-Family And Multifamily Construction: Bring On The Supply Just As Population Growth Slows To A Crawl
data.description:
This is precisely what this overpriced and frozen housing market needs.

In 2025, construction was started on 1.36 million privately owned housing units, down just a hair from a year ago: 943,000 single-family housing units and 416,000 multifamily housing units (condos and rental apartments), according to Census Bureau data today.

About 2.3 people occupy a housing unit on average. So the 1.36 million housing units that were started in 2025 would provide a home, when completed, for 3.1 million additional people. But the US population increased by only 1.78 million people in the 12 months through mid-2025; and for the 12 month through mid-2026, it is projected to grow by only 756,000 people; and growth might further decline in the following years, according to the Census Bureau’s estimates.

In the years before 2009, the US population grew by 2.5-2.8 million people per year. In the 12 months through mid-2025, population growth was half that. For the 12 months through mid-2026, population growth will be between one-third to one-quarter of that. This continued flood of new housing supply amid dramatically slowing population growth is precisely what this overpriced and frozen housing market needs.

Single-family housing units

Construction starts of privately owned single-family housing units, at 943,000 in 2025, were down by 6.9% from 2024 but roughly on the same pace as in 2023, as homebuilders pulled back amid very high inventories of new homes for sale and falling prices.

Falling prices of new single-family homes: For example, Lennar (LEN), one of the largest homebuilders in the US, has been trying to maintain its sales volume by piling on incentives, cutting prices, giving up a substantial part of its big-fat pandemic-era profit margins, and building at lower costs.

As a result, the average price of the homes that it sold has plunged; and for Q1 2026, it guided the average price down further, to a range of $365,000-375,000 net of incentives. The mid-point ($370,000 blue in the chart) would be the lowest sales price since Q1 2017, and down by 25% from the peak in Q3 2022 ($491,000).

Soaring inventories of new single-family homes: Inventories of completed single-family homes for sale by homebuilders have piled up to the highest level since 2009, to 124,000, per the Census Bureau’s latest data available through October. And homebuilders are responding to it with lower average selling prices. Bring on the supply!

Multifamily housing units

Construction starts of privately owned multifamily housing units (rentals and condos in buildings of 2 or more units) rose by 17.4% to 416,000 units in 2025.

Outside of the years 2021-2023, the 2025 pace was the highest since 1986. Bring on the supply!

In many markets, there has been a flood of higher-end rental apartments and condos coming on the market. The bigger projects take years to plan and build. Vacancies have been rising as these units came on the market. Many projects that had been planned but not started became stalled due to lack of financing as the CRE depression spread around. Asking rents have been edging down in some of those markets (but rising in others). There have already been a number of large multifamily defaults, and the multifamily CMBS delinquency rate has surged to over 7%, from 1% in 2024.

About 95% of these multifamily housing units were in buildings with 5 or more units. Only about 5% were in small buildings with 2-4 units.

Construction starts of housing units in small multifamily buildings with 2-4 units accounted for just 5% of all multifamily construction starts, at 19,100 starts in 2025.

But the pace has accelerated by 66% since 2015 (11,500 starts), sort of a rising-from-the-ashes moment, after that segment had nearly vanished in 2008. This is a playground that is accessible to mom-and-pop developers and landlords.

In densely populated urban cores, higher-end multifamily housing units (condos and apartments) are about the only type of housing that is getting built.

With higher-end apartments, builders are targeting “renters of choice” - people who could afford to buy a house out in the suburbs, but want to live in a modern high-rise in a central location with all the amenities and conveniences, a short commute, possibly an easy social life, and the feeling of being in the middle of things, topped off by the flexibility that rentals provide.

Developers cannot target lower-income tenants or buyers because the construction costs just don’t pencil out, unless the buildings are subsidized - and there is that too.

Renters with less-stellar incomes generally have to make do with renting apartments or condos in older buildings in less desirable areas, sometimes in run-down buildings with five decades of deferred maintenance.

Single-family construction has been spreading further out from urban cores - the urban sprawl that is everywhere. This includes “build to rent” single-family developments, designed and built specifically as rentals mostly for “renters of choice” by large single-family landlords or by builders that then fill the developments with tenants and sell the income-producing project to large landlords, pension funds, and other funds.

These build-to-rent developments have common amenities, a leasing and maintenance office, and are more efficient to manage for the landlord than individual houses scattered across their market, and they’ve been selling those scatter units here and there at sky-high profits for the past two years. Read... The Biggest Single-Family Rental Landlords and Multifamily Landlords in the US: Big Shifts Underway

data.title: Blackstone Mortgage Trust's Dividend Yield Nears Double Digits As Impairments Dip
data.description:
Blackstone Mortgage Trust (BXMT) is paying out a well-covered near double-digit dividend yield from an aggressively diversified global commercial real estate credit portfolio that is seeing both revenue and distributable earnings strength. BXMT is huge, with its credit portfolio holding a principal balance of $18.16 billion as of the end of its fiscal 2025 fourth quarter. This was spread across 131 loans, with a maximum 2.5-year maturity and an all-in yield of 3.39%. The dividend is the prize, and BXMT last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.47 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $1.88 per share annualized for a 9.64% dividend yield.

BXMT's bull case is built around its incredibly well-diversified CRE portfolio, with around 48% of its portfolio collateral as of the end of its fourth quarter coming from non-U.S. markets. This was further diversified across asset classes, with Office properties forming 27% of the portfolio, Multifamily at 26%, and Industrial at 24%. U.S. CRE exposure is further broadly diversified across states, with California its largest exposure at 10% of the portfolio. This depth of diversification acts as a healthy hedge to a CRE market that's been discombobulated by stubbornly high long-term U.S. Treasury rates in light of Fed rate cuts.

The mREIT reported fourth-quarter total revenue of $159.3 million, up 39.2% over $114.45 million in its year-ago comp. The jump in revenue was mainly driven by a 538% increase in revenue from owned real estate, negating what was a 17.37% year-over-year dip in net income from loans to $83.92 million from $101.56 million. Bears would be right to point out that this jump in real estate owned income reflects BXMT taking control of the collateral on credit that's turned sour, and that the dip in net income from its credit portfolio reflects a setback in the mREIT's ability to maintain its dividend distribution at its current level. This bearish risk is heightened by total expenses that jump by 94% year-over-year to $108.06 million, driven by expenses from owned real estate.

Dividend Coverage, Balance Sheet Health, And Credit Quality

GAAP net income came in at $0.24 per share, up 3 cents from the year-ago comp. However, distributable earnings were negative at $2.07 per share after charge-offs, and $0.51 per share after adjusting for charge-offs. This means the mREIT is covering its dividend by 108.5%. The level of coverage improved sequentially with distributable earnings prior to charge-offs expanding from $0.48 per share in the third quarter and by 20% from the first quarter of 2025. BXMT cut its dividend by 24.2% in the summer of 2024, a move that shareholders would be keen to avoid happening again. Total returns for the mREIT are running at 4.77% over the last 1 year, slightly underperforming the 6.20% total return from the iShares Mortgage Real Estate ETF (REM). BXMT's dividend yield has trended lower from a peak of north of 14% in 2023 to its current range. This reflects material progress by the mREIT in addressing impaired loans and the perception of improving CRE credit quality headwinds.

The mREIT's loan portfolio was 99% performing as of the end of the fourth quarter, closing $7 billion of total investments in 2025, with a focus on multifamily and industrial loans at 85% of this total. The mREIT held a current expected credit loss reserve ("CECL") of $284.44 million at the end of the fourth quarter, down from $733.94 million a year ago. Crucially, BXMT's loan portfolio performance was up a full 400 basis points from its first quarter, with impaired loans down 96% from a peak of $2.3 billion in the third quarter of 2024 to $100 million as of the end of the fourth quarter. The mREIT also held cash and cash equivalents of $452.53 million at the end of the fourth quarter, up roughly 40% from its year-ago comp.

This improvement in its underlying portfolio has been driven by a pivot towards Multifamily and Industrial CRE, as well as taking ownership of impaired loans. BXMT is managed by a subsidiary of Blackstone (BX), one of the largest alternative asset managers, with around $1.3 trillion in assets under management. The mREIT reported a fourth quarter book value of $20.75 per share, a figure that includes $1.76 per share of CECL reserves and $0.47 per share of accumulated depreciation of owned real estate. This means the common shares at $19.51 per share are swapping hands at a small 6% discount to book value per share as of the end of the fourth quarter.

Conclusion

The mREIT's current dividend yield provides a 554 basis point spread to the U.S. 10-year Treasury rate (US10Y) at 4.10%. This is around 41% above the mREIT's tightest level and comes with an improving credit quality profile. The dividend is also safe at its current level. I am rating BXMT a Buy.

data.title: ETHA Is Not Ethereum: The Structural Cost Of Owning The Wrapper
data.description:
Introduction

Owning the iShares Ethereum Trust ETF (ETHA) is different from owning Ethereum itself (Note: I will refer to Ethereum as ETH since that is the native crypto symbol for it. This should not be confused with the Mini ETF, whose ticker is ETH). The difference is not trivially one of convenience and custody. It is much more structural. Not only does ETHA package ETH inside a brokerage wrapper, but it also transforms a protocol asset into a regulated financial instrument. This changes many things.

Ethereum is a network asset. You can trade Ethereum 24/7, all day. ETH does proof-of-stake (PoS) validation. Its return has two components: what it gains from price appreciation and the PoS yield.

ETHA, on the other hand, is a Delaware trust. It holds ETH, and returns come only from price performance, after expenses. Like any normal market security, it trades only during market hours. There’s a 0.25% sponsor fee, and ETHA has neither any distributable income nor is involved in staking. It tracks the CME CF Ether-Dollar Reference Rate—New York Variant, a regulated benchmark rate.

I mentioned staking, but the differences go beyond that. These differences impact where returns come from. As we will also discuss shortly, they have an effect on volatility, counterparty exposure, and how productive capital remains when idle. If you are a direct ETH investor, you get both risks and rewards at the protocol level. You get no protocol participation risks if you are owning ETHA, but you get other benefits. These include benefits due to ETHA’s custodial structure, authorized participants, involvement of regulators in its oversight, and basic brokerage access.

Return Composition: Price-Only vs. Network-Productive Capital

One key way ETH is more productive than ETHA is because it does network validation under proof-of-stake. This produces a reward in token for its holder. So you are not just sitting and waiting for price appreciation—as ETHA does—but you are actually working your investment. ETHA doesn’t do this because it wants to remain within a strict regulatory framework. It also wants to avoid certain operational and legal risks. These risks are things like a strict lock-up period for ETH, slashing risks, and so on that hurt ETH’s liquidity. So ETHA relies only on price appreciation for its returns.

ETHA sponsor fees came to $11.84 million in the first nine months of 2025. That is against an AUM of $6.4 billion. That said, those fees do come out of the trust’s ether holdings, so, over time, they reduce NAV.

ETH itself is highly volatile, and price appreciation of 70-100% in a year could occur. If ETH goes up 80% in a year, a 0.25% fee and even the staking yield could be marginal relative to those gains. An investor wanting to avoid the complexity of direct Ethereum investing may still be able to successfully participate in its price gains by simply holding ETHA. However, in flat markets, the fee starts to bite.

So, say in a flat market ETH only goes up 6% annually, and the staking yield is 3%; then a direct staker is making 9% before costs. ETHA’s return during the same period would be 6% minus the fees, or 5.75%. So the annual spread is 3.25%, which could become a substantial difference over longer periods.

Tax Structure: Income Recognition vs. Deferred Realization

There are key differences in how the taxman treats these two sources of returns. Staking rewards of direct ETH holders are treated as ordinary income. So, whether you sell ETH or not, you pay taxes. Longer-term appreciation from those rewarded tokens becomes capital gains, so you pay capital gains tax on those too.

For ETHA holders, the tax situation is different. The ETF does not distribute income and does not stake. So you pay no periodic taxes. You only pay taxes when you sell ETHA. Until then, gains or losses are unrealized.

In the earlier example, only the staking portion of the 3.25% pre-tax spread is taxed annually, depending on your tax bracket. If you are paying 24% taxes, the spread comes to 2.53%. At 37%, it comes to 2.14%. This is still a gap, but it isn’t as much as earlier.

So we see deferral has some economic value for taxable investors. For retirement accounts, however, tax-sheltered accounts paying 0% taxes on income, the advantage disappears, and ETH becomes more rewarding.

Liquidity & Market Structure: 24/7 Protocol Asset vs. Exchange-Hour Instrument

Ethereum trades across many exchanges around the world. It goes up and down in real time. Unlike ETHA, it does not keep US market hours only. When US markets close, ETHA trading stops, but ETH trading doesn’t. This is a crucial difference.

So if you are holding ETHA, and the underlying ETH makes major weekend moves, you can’t get into the game until Monday. This happens routinely—crypto is notorious for making sharp moves outside US trading hours.

Another point: while ETHA does have a large asset base of between $6 billion and $9 billion (the difference is based on how it is being reported), ETH trades across dozens of global exchanges with $19-20 billion in daily turnover. That’s a 2-3x multiple of ETHA.

While these are constraints on ETHA, there are some benefits to them. One of these is that during trading hours, bid-ask spreads are tighter for ETHA. This helps brokerage investors with more predictable execution and settlement.

Counterparty & Custody Risk: Protocol Risk vs. Institutional Risk

Native ETH has various risks peculiar to protocol-level assets. Investors need to manage private keys, which could cause losses, especially if an asset is self-custodial. Unintentional improper validator behaviour could make you lose rewards. Intentional or malicious behaviour, although rare, could cause slashing penalties.

Another type of protocol-level risk is exposure to smart contracts. Here, a software bug could exploit drained funds. In some situations, especially outside the secure ether ecosystem, you could even see the staking pool contract getting compromised.

In a regulated setup like ETHA, these risks do not exist. Here, your ETF’s ether is held directly by a custodian (here, Coinbase Prime), so there are institutional safeguards. However, risks are not eliminated entirely; they just shift somewhere else. Here, with ETHA, you must rely on sponsors, custodians, and authorized participants. Each of these elements must work properly in the capital market for the account to clear and settle correctly.

Capital Efficiency & Financial Integration: Brokerage Asset vs. On-Chain Asset

Ethereum is an asset that can be made to work. It can be productive. You can deploy it in a decentralized manner and use it as collateral in network lending protocols. It can be added to liquidity pools or staked. You can basically use your owned on-chain ETH to earn more ETH.

This is not something you can do with ETHA. You do hold beneficial ownership in the trust that holds ETH, but what you own is not ETH but this trust. You cannot use those shares within decentralized systems. You cannot stake them, lend them on-chain, or deploy them in smart contracts.

On the other hand, you can margin ETHA on brokerage accounts, you can use it as collateral for ordinary loans, and there’s a busy option market so you can write covered calls, buy protective puts, and so on. In short, you can do financial engineering with the ETH-backed ETF that you couldn’t standardly do with native ETH (there are non-standard ways to do some of these things).

So it is not a simple trade-off on yield. You are also exchanging on-chain deployability for the flexibility of brokerage holding.

Conclusion

The foregoing discussion should have made clear who should go native with ETH and who should stick to ETHA. Broadly, there are three situations where owning ETH is more profitable. One, if you are highly tech-savvy and know how to go on the network, manage private keys, and stake your coins, ETH is for you. Two, if you are putting your money in tax-sheltered accounts, ETHA offers no tax advantage but only the fee drag. Third, if ether is going through a sustained flat market, then the price advantage of ETH outweighs the structural advantages of ETHA. In all these situations, owning ETH is the better deal.

data.title: Opendoor Technologies Q4 FY2025 Earnings Review: Progress Should Continue
data.description:
DISCLAIMER: This note is intended for U.S. recipients only and, in particular, is not directed at, nor intended to be relied upon by, any UK recipients. Nothing in this note is intended to be investment advice, nor should it be relied upon to make investment decisions. Please read our full disclaimer here.

The Early Stages Of Success

Corporate turnarounds are difficult. More difficult than you think if you’ve never led one or been involved in one at a top-team level. All the things that the junior grumblerati have been complaining about for years are, in fact, things because they are too difficult to fix. Cost bases take time and money to right-size, and the revenue line is even harder.

There are two preconditions that can raise the probability of a successful turnaround. The first is a complete collapse in confidence around the company and its stock, and the second is a new-broom CEO stepping in after the collapse, one whose confidence inspires the remaining ranks to get to work a little earlier each morning, work a little harder, and leave a little later, all with a sense of pride in helping the company to turn the corner.

Opendoor Technologies Inc. (OPEN) has both those key steps in place. The stock had cratered to $1 before the turnaround began, and the new CEO comes from the no.2 role at Shopify Inc. (SHOP), where he was said to be highly regarded.

The stock has started to shape up.

The lows earlier this week, I think, can be considered a Wave 4 low; it was struck right around the price of the Wave 1 high on the above chart, and it also found support thus far at the .618 retrace of the whole move up from the July lows.

If I have this right, I think it means the stock still has a shot at between $11-13 before a major correction. The initial earnings reaction was good, with the stock up materially overnight vs. Thursday’s close. We have to see where Friday closes to get a meaningful sense of how the market has digested earnings. That is a more important price than the low-volume post- and then pre-market machinations.

As regards reality? I think these are the essentials:

  • Revenue continues to decline, but I suspect this is just a lagging indicator of the work being done at the company. The company beat expectations, which is a start.
  • Cash flow is very, very strong, driven primarily by the reduction in inventory of property. In some ways, declining inventory is bad; the company does have to have something to sell, but the likelihood is that, for now, clearing out "bad" / old inventory and having cash on the books is a good thing.
  • The balance sheet is in perfectly good shape for now as a result. It may need financing should the company decide to really step up.
  • The stock is cheap in my view, at around 1x TTM revenue / 5x TTM unlevered pretax FCF.

Here are the numbers:

Here's the inventory unwind (reminder: a good thing for now):

And finally, the valuation:

I believe progress will continue. I am long the stock and may add to it further today.

Alex King, CEO, Cestrian Capital Research, Inc - 20 Feb 2026.

data.title: Chipotle: Not Enough Value On The Menu Or In The Stock
data.description:
Thesis and Primer

After suffering stalled and shrinking sales that sparked a sustained slide in share prices, McDonald’s (NYSE:MCD) pivoted back towards emphasizing value. Not long after, sales began to tick up again, and the stock stabilized and even began to post some gains (and has performed especially well in recent weeks). A few other companies, like Domino’s, have also been able to stabilize and increase sales by emphasizing value, though pressures on margins and other headwinds have made for tough sledding in the fast and fast-casual restaurant scene. Against this backdrop, Chipotle’s (NYSE:CMG) seeming disregard for value offerings strikes me as an especially risky play. I rated CMG as a sell back in May 2025, and while the slide has improved some valuation metrics, overall I still believe CMG simply doesn't offer good value for investors.

I’d also keep an eye on GLP-1 and its impact on fast and fast-casual restaurants, although Chipotle may be a bit better positioned here than many competitors. I’d also at least consider potentially emerging AI-driven layoffs at large corporate offices and especially tech companies, as these are the types of customers Chipotle is emphasizing. GLP-1 strikes me as an emerging, legitimate threat, but AI-driven layoffs are more of a speculative threat that I’d want to study more closely before adding too much weight to.

Beware Of Leaning Too Hard On Customers

Chipotle CEO Scott Boatwright sparked a bit of an uproar during the company’s recent earnings call, noting that 60% of its customers were from households with $100,000 or more in annual income and that he was confident that the company could “lean into” that group to “drive transaction performance.” Many netizens took this as a sign that the company would look to drive prices up, if anything.

Boatwright also told Yahoo Finance that he had no interest in offering a value menu like the one McDonald’s has been leaning into to rekindle sales growth. Among other things, he argued that he didn’t want to devalue the company’s offerings. I can understand not wanting to get pulled into a price war, as they’re often not good for margins, but consumers are becoming especially price sensitive, and we are seeing evidence that even wealthier households are becoming more price conscious.

Walmart has seen sales from wealthier households notch steady gains over the last few years, likely driven by cost-saving measures, with the company reporting in November that households earning more than $100,000 were driving three-quarters of the company’s growth. High-income households have also shown an increasing willingness to buy private labels, and both Aldi’s and Costco have been seeing strong engagement with affluent customers. Of course, CMG is not competing directly with grocery stores for most of its business, but shifting habits among affluent households suggest that said consumers are increasingly price sensitive. Further, McDonald’s also reported a strong increase in sales from wealthy customers this past fall.

On top of the above, food consumption overall could come under pressure as GLP-1 drugs become increasingly popular. Chipotle’s position as a healthier alternative to fast-food restaurants like McDonald’s may soften some of the blow, but a study found that households with at least one GLP-1 user spent 8% less on fast food, coffee shops, and limited-service restaurants. GLP-1 drugs tend to reduce cravings, so price-conscious consumers already wanting to protect their wallets may prove especially resistant to giving in to cravings for Chipotle and other expensive offerings. By the way, GLP-1 users tend to skew towards being more affluent.

This is more speculative and food for thought at this point, but I believe that Chipotle’s focus on higher-income households could leave it more vulnerable to AI-driven white-collar job loss. I believe we’ll find that those $100,000 households Chipotle relies on will be disproportionately made up of white-collar workers, who could be among the first impacted if (big if) sustained AI-driven job loss is as bad as some fear. Keep in mind that one of Chipotle’s secret sauces in its rapid expansion was targeting the corporate lunch crowd, and many locations lack the drive-thrus favored by blue-collar workers on lunch breaks.

CMG Fundamentals and Valuation

All in all, I’m not particularly impressed by Chipotle’s fundamentals. I believe comparative analysis is one of the most useful approaches. While I’ve mentioned McDonald’s a few times and said the company is important for my thesis, since it uses a franchise-based model, it’s hard to make direct comparisons. Instead, I am going to use Brinker International (NYSE:EAT), which owns the corporate-managed Chili’s chain, which in turn has become a value king of sorts.

A long share price slide has improved the valuation somewhat since I last looked at the company, but I still think it’s rather expensive. Right off the bat, CMG’s 33.38 GAAP P/E (TTM) is something you’d expect to see with a hot tech stock, not a fast-casual restaurant. EAT’s 15.09 P/E ratio is far more reasonable. With the Price/Sales ratio (TTM), CMG is a hefty 4.25 to EAT’s 1.28. It’s important not to put too much weight into just a few indicators; however, these valuation metrics are very tough to swallow for a mature company, and I’d want to see double-digit revenue growth and would probably need it to exceed 20%. But alas, Chipotle’s YOY revenue growth is a mere 5.41%, while EAT’s is an enticing 17.88%.

Where CMG looks best is arguably profitability. The 40.10% gross profit margin trounces EAT's 18.69%. The net income margin is solid at 12.88% as well, and well above EAT’s 7.98%. All in all, these margins are quite high for a non-franchisee restaurant chain. The problem here, however, goes back to my thesis: you’re going to get such margins with hefty markups, and that in turn, hints at a less-than-ideal value proposition for customers. Given the high valuation and lack of omph with other metrics, I suspect many investors are putting too much into the company’s brand value and are placing too much emphasis on profitability that is going to be very hard to maintain given the overall state of the consumer and economy.

CMG doesn't offer dividends. Seeking Alpha’s quant tools give CMG mediocre-to-poor marks outside of everything but profitability (which is arguably a liability). Overall, Seeking Alpha’s analysts are more upbeat than I am, mostly assigning holds or buys, but I’m simply not seeing enough value here to feel optimistic about.

Why I Could Be Wrong

Chipotle has been on a long slide, and its valuation has improved since I last looked at it. While I think it’s still a bit overpriced, markets in general are rather expensive right now. There could be value here I’m simply not seeing. Further, while some economic indicators have been flashing troubling signs, overall, the economy remains resilient. If inflation cools and wages continue to rise, people may end up with more to spend, boosting companies like Chipotle.

Key Takeaway: Not Enough Value In This Stock

Ultimately, consumers remain under pressure, and I believe this will have an outsized impact on companies that don’t prioritize value. I understand why some companies, like Chipotle, are wary of cutting too close to the bone with promotions and the like, but especially in this economy, I believe this is increasingly necessary. I would need to see much stronger growth while also sustaining margins to justify the current valuation.

data.title: Arcutis After The Double: Hypergrowth Peaks, Compounding Remains
data.description:
In June last year, I had issued a Buy rating for Arcutis Biotherapeutics (ARQT). At that time, Arcutis had meaningfully de-risked its story. ZORYVE had secured multiple approvals. Early revenue traction was visible. Partnerships suggested potential reach beyond dermatology specialists. Cash burn was also narrowing, and breakeven by 2026 appeared possible.

Valuations at ~4.8x EV to revenues were comfortable for a company with reduced binary risks and strong growth optionality ahead. The only risk in my mind was underwriting execution. The thesis was that regulatory clarity plus commercial scaling would re-rate the stock. That thesis has worked favorably, and shares are now trading at double the levels we saw in June. I find several factors today pointing to much of the thesis having played out. The forward expectations look slower, and the risk-reward asymmetry is less favorable. Hence, the thesis shifts to a hold—this is not a downgrade because the path ahead still looks positive, only slower. This Hold implies no chasing with further fresh investments, and for those who entered at the lows, some profit booking and trimming of positions.

Thesis Review - Positives

ZORYVE has expanded its approval set with some pediatric expansions and additional formulations (e.g., scalp/body or certain age brackets). The transition from approval to execution was already made by June last year, in my view. What the additional approvals mean is an expanding market to cater to.

During this phase of additional validation and execution, revenues have shot up as expected. From ~$65m at the time of writing the June thesis, Q3 2025 revenue has grown by over 50% over just two quarters. The current revenue run rate is ~$400m. The quarter also saw Arcutis report its first quarterly net income. Cash flow breakeven was expected in 2026. Management now indicates that sales growth and expense discipline support reaching the positive cash flow by Q4 2025 itself. The improving profitability and cash flow situation implies that cash burn, one of the concerns still open in June last year, appears less relevant now.

For the improvements made, a jump in EV to forward revenues from ~4.8x to ~6.8x does not appear to be a huge valuation jump. I see it as a fair rerating, with earnings upgrades visibly doing significant heavy lifting to double share prices.

Long-Term Supports - No Immediate Catalyst

The steroid conversion potential was visible in June and has strengthened. At present, ZORYVE controls roughly 3% of topical steroid prescriptions in dermatology. In Q3, management stated its long-term target market share as 15-20%. Since percentage gain translates to almost $150m, we are talking about a market size of $2.25-3b in the long term. How penetrative ZORYVE becomes could determine how fast it reaches there, but there are supporting factors why this is not an unattainable North Star.

Doctors and patients are becoming more cautious about long-term steroid use. A gradual change in prescriptions toward ZORYVE therefore looks more likely, although such a regime change could be gradual, not immediate. ZORYVE, as an alternative path that is tested safe and is in commercial use, presents that route. However, regime shifts in prescriptive trends often take 5-10 years to fully translate. Steroids have been in use for decades, so inertia is high. And therefore, ZORYVE's path ahead is supported but not necessarily exciting immediately in 2026. There are risks to that long-term story too, as long-term safety and efficacy narratives generally take years to form - any dent in that journey could mean revisiting those targets, although so far, so good.

Steroid conversion is the most immediate catalyst I see, and that looks like a positive slope ahead, not a spike. The long-term thesis appears well-positioned, but near-term catalysts are not indicated. The long-term thesis has other optionalities at play too.

Arcutis is running small Phase II proof-of-concept studies in new conditions like vitiligo and hidradenitis suppurativa. These involve only about 20 patients each, so they are low-cost and low-risk from a capital standpoint. If successful, they can plug into the existing sales and distribution setup. However, even positive data here does not materially change 2026 revenue. ARQ-234 adds another optionality as a systemic biologic. But timelines to certifications are longer, so any meaningful revenue addition, if successful, does not impact 2026 or even 2027 revenue. Each of the Phase I and II timelines could take more than a year, and then FDA review starts - so we are definitely not counting this as a near-term support. This is a pure optionality at the moment that raises the long-term ceiling.

The short-term lack of catalysts is visible in the product revenue expectations set between $455m and $470m in 2026. At the midpoint, that is only about a ~15% lift from the revenue run rate of $400m implied in Q3 2025. That is not a negative slowdown from the triple-digit headline growth numbers we are seeing now in the early stages - it is expected, as penetration from current levels will likely take time.

The hypergrowth high point may be well past us already, and even a sizeable guidance issue in Q4 could still mean a confirmation of the slower road ahead from here.

Potential Path Ahead

Even going by management's own assessment of an implied long-term target of ~$3b (15-20% market share midpoint), the compounding from here looks a lot slower than what has been delivered in just over 8 months. Unless Arcutis achieves its long-term targets ahead of the 5-10 year expectations needed for creating significant inroads into a decades-old steroid market, a mid-aggressive 7-year period required to reach these targets translates to moderate returns as per realistic good-case assumptions.

Looking at Arcutis' profitability profile and current high gross margins compared to the sector median (superior pricing power potentially), we can assume an EBIT margin of ~30% as a reasonable good case (far above the sector median of ~6%). This also broadly aligns with ~50% of revenue as SG&A costs and, say, ~10% in R&D costs at maturity - a fair assumption for a high gross margin specialty dermatology franchise.

Now, ~30% EBIT margins imply a ~$900m EBIT in 2032. Applying a ~20x EV to EBIT multiple (sector median), the EV works out as $18b. Discounting back to today using a 12% discount rate, the present value of that EV translates to ~$8b. In this successful commercial scaling scenario, the CAGR growth of EV from today's $3.2b works out to ~14%. This is where the thesis is both well-supported in terms of long-term compounding with execution risks on one hand and no near-term excitement after a ~100% rally.

Downgrading and Q4 Outlook

With Q4 likely to come with a confirmation of a slowing growth year in 2026, I feel it is a good time to slow down and await further positive developments in Q4 and subsequent quarters. Management has already indicated GTN to be weaker in Q4 compared to its tailwind role in sequential improvement in Q3 sales.

Q4 will also be the first full quarter of the foam product scaling. After the optimism that surrounds a launch quarter, if Q4 disappoints, expectations will be recalibrated. The overall downgrade before the quarterly results is not speculative in my view, as, apart from a more visible GTN weakness, growth deceleration as the base expands is mathematically expected and already being observed (as 2026 guidance). Therefore, Arcutis is best viewed as a Hold. It does have a path to strong compounding ahead, but the risk-reward asymmetry now is not the same as it was in June, by a distance.

I would re-evaluate fresh investments if revenue trends show materially stronger than expected trends - something like at least a 25% sequential rise despite weaker GTN (similar to the sequential rise in Q2, with the GTN tailwind), on the back of strong prescription growth and a guidance above $500m for the next year. That higher but reasonable bar stems from the higher valuations we are sitting on today. Any pullback toward ~5x (closer to June valuations, with earnings upgrade still intact) could also trigger considering fresh investments.

data.title: FPA Queens Road Small Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Performance Review
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the FPA Queens Road Small Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary.

Top Contributors

  • Fabrinet (FN) is a contract manufacturer specializing in optical networking equipment and modules. This is complex work at small scales, and Fabrinet dominates its niche. The company has experienced impressive historical revenue growth and growing operating margins. Fabrinet’s highest bandwidth products are finding increasing demand in data centers, especially those data centers used to train artificial intelligence models. In 2023, Fabrinet disclosed that Nvidia is a 10% customer. FN’s stock price has more than doubled since April on rising expectations for data center spending. We believe such spending, as capitalized in FN’s stock price, may have gotten ahead of itself and have been trimming. But the need for high bandwidth networking will continue, Fabrinet dominates its core telecom and data center markets and has prudently diversified into a handful of other niches that reward precise assembly at small scale. We believe Fabrinet will be a “compounder” for many years, and we continue to hold a position.
  • InterDigital (IDCC) is a R&D organization that develops and acquires wireless communication and video patents. The company has a history of strong financial performance, opportunistically buys back shares, and pays a modest dividend. InterDigital has been successfully renewing its wireless licensing agreements (Apple in 2022, Samsung in 2023) and has a growing stream of recurring licensing revenues across consumer electronics, internet of things (IoT), and automotive customers. CEO Liren Chen joined the company in 2021 from Qualcomm and has been hiring other former Qualcomm managers. The company’s share price has increased over the past year due to growing revenue, profitability, and buybacks. We like the company’s long-term prospects but began trimming the Fund’s position this year on valuation.
  • REV Group (REVG) is a specialty vehicle manufacturer. Most of the company’s value is in its municipal business where REV Group makes fire trucks and ambulances. The company has been reporting significant backlog and pricing growth in concert with Pierce Manufacturing (owned by Osh Kosh (OSK), another Fund holding), their primary competitor in the fire truck business. CEO Mark Skonieczny has led a significant operational restructuring, sold off the less profitable bus business, and used the proceeds to buy back stock at attractive prices. The stock price has followed improving results and performed well.
  • UGI Corp (UGI) is a well-run gas and electric utility in Western Pennsylvania and West Virginia. The company also owns a sizable, regulated pipeline business, a large propane distribution business in Europe, and Amerigas, the U.S.’s largest propane distributor. UGI has been redirecting cash flow to pay down debt at Amerigas and the utility holding company. We are pleased with the company’s improved financial position, green shoots at Amerigas and the upgrade to UGI’s long-term EPS growth rate announced during fiscal Q4 earnings.
  • CSG Systems (CSGS) makes software that manages customer relationships and billing for telecom companies. Under CEO Brian Shephard, the company has done an admirable job adding geographic and customer diversification. On October 29, NEC Corp, the parent of competitor Netcracker, announced that it was acquiring CSGS for $80.70 per share.

Bottom Contributors

  • PVH (PVH) is an apparel company that owns the Tommy Hilfiger and Calvin Klein brands globally. Most of PVH’s earnings come from Europe, where the Tommy and Calvin brands are considered “almost luxury” and where PVH has demonstrated high single-digit organic growth with pricing power over the decade preceding Covid. CEO Stefan Larsson has done an excellent job revitalizing the company and improving margins at PVH’s moribund U.S. operations. But, comparable revenue has been roughly flat over the last four quarters as the company bumped into weaker global consumer spending and actively cleaned up its channel inventory in Europe. Investors are skittish about tariffs and, in 2025, PVH’s inclusion on China’s “Unreliable Entity” list which threatens the roughly 15% of the company’s profitability that comes from China. But PVH’s sales and sourcing are globally diversified, and the company uses its prodigious cash flow to buy back shares. We think shares are cheap at roughly 6x trailing earnings.
  • Upbound Group (UPBD) is a sub-prime lender that employs a rent-to-own model. The company has two primary segments: Rent-a-Center, which focuses on furniture and appliances through physical stores; and Acima, which offers last look financing through associated retailers including electronics and tire shops. Sub-prime consumers have struggled this year and Upbound’s operating results have ticked down. The company has been slow to de-lever after purchasing Acima in 2021 and made another incremental acquisition on Jan. 31, when it bought Brigit, an app which charges subscription fees to access payday lending. UPBD stock is cheap at less than five times forward earnings. But we have been slow to add, given the company’s leverage and questions about its business model.
  • RLI Corp (RLI) is a specialty property and casualty insurer. The company is extremely selective in writing business. Its diversified lines include niche-y areas such as public and school busses, Hawaii homeowners, and surety. RLI’s unique culture, incentive structures and willingness to walk away from unprofitable business have historically led to exceptional financial performance. From 2015 through 2024, the company’s combined ratio has averaged 89.3% and its ROE has averaged 16.6% despite being weighed down by excess capital. We have owned RLI since 2011 and the stock usually commands a premium valuation – we are pleased to be able to add to our position at roughly 20x this year’s earnings.
  • ServisFirst Bancshares (SFBS) is a conservatively run lending franchise helmed by Tom Broughton. Tom hires local bankers but doesn’t build branches – this allows for best-in-class efficiency metrics while maintaining a strong and conservative lending culture. Return on equity (ROE) and average earnings per share growth were near 20% for the 10 years through 2024 – very attractive for a conservative, plain vanilla commercial lender. We think that SFBS trailed the regional bank indices in 2025 because of its concentrated exposure to South-Eastern real estate, a large Q3 non-accrual for which the bank obtained additional collateral and disappointing Q3 loan growth. We have followed the company for years and are extremely comfortable continuing to hold SFBS in our top 10 holdings.
  • Advance Auto Parts (AAP) is an auto parts distributor that sells to both professional repair technicians and retail customers. The company is struggling through a difficult turnaround and sits firmly in our “opportunistic value” bucket. But the balance sheet is in good shape, new management has articulated a credible restructuring plan and the company’s mid-term guidance for 7% operating margins is reasonable compared to competitors and Advance Auto’s own history. Results have been inconsistent as management works through the restructuring of Advance Auto’s distribution, inventory, merchandising and store operations.

Important Information

This Commentary is for informational and discussion purposes only and does not constitute, and should not be construed as, an offer or solicitation for the purchase or sale of any securities, products or services discussed, and neither does it provide investment advice. Any such offer or solicitation shall only be made pursuant to the Fund’s Prospectus, which supersedes the information contained herein in its entirety. Glossary of Terms

Downside-capture ratio is used to evaluate how well an investment manager performed relative to an index during periods when that index has fallen. The ratio is calculated by dividing the manager’s returns by the returns of the index during the down market, and multiplying that factor by 100. BPS or basis points; 1 bps is equal to 0.01%.

Earnings per share (EPS) is calculated as a company’s profit divided by the outstanding shares of its common stock.

Expected earnings are an estimate for a company’s future quarterly or annual earnings per share.

Forward earnings are an estimate of the next period’s earnings of a company, usually through the completion of the current fiscal year and sometimes to the following fiscal year.

Free Cash Flow represents the cash a company can generate after required investment to maintain or expand its asset base.

Margin of Safety – Buying with a “margin of safety” is when a security is purchased at a discount to the portfolio manager’s estimate of its intrinsic value. Buying a security with a margin of safety is designed to protect against permanent capital loss in the case of an unexpected event or analytical mistake. A purchase made with a margin of safety does not guarantee the security will not decline in price.

Market Cycle Performance is defined as a period that contains a decline of at least 20% from the previous market peak over at least a two-month period and a rebound to establish a new peak above the prior market peak.

Price/Earnings Ratio (P/E) is the price of a stock divided by its earnings per share. Percentages are based on the absolute number of shares. P/E ex-Neg EPS is Price/Earnings ex negative earnings per share. P/E, average P/E, or P/E (trailing) is based on the last 12 months of actual earnings. P/E, next 12 months or P/E (forward) utilizes forward earnings expectations over the next 12 months to calculate the ratio.

Return on Equity (ROE) measures a corporation’s profitability by revealing how much profit a company generates with the money shareholders have invested.

data.title: Amazon Stock Still Makes No Sense
data.description:
On Feb 5, 2026, Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) reported its fourth-quarter and full-year 2025 earnings—and beat expectations. Revenue for the quarter came in at $213.39 billion, while earnings per share (diluted) were $1.92. And for FY 2025, Amazon pulled in $716.92 billion on the top line and delivered $7.17 per share, topping market estimates by 0.29% and 1.35%, respectively.

But despite it, AMZN stock dropped ~8% since just before the release. Zoom out YTD, or over the last 12 months, and the picture isn't much better.

For a company that's growing both its top and bottom line at a clip that would impress most any other shareholder, all while developing a suite of high-margin products, it's failed to win investor support. And that doesn't quite make sense... yet.

However, given its growth drivers ahead and the recent dip, my Strong Buy rating for Amazon remains in place.

What was in Amazon’s fourth-quarter and 2025 financials?

In Q4, Amazon reported revenue was up 14%, reaching $213.4 billion. You'll also note that the growth was backed by double-digit increases across all segments.

Note: USD in billions.

Notably, AWS grew 24% to $35.6 billion, representing a 20% increase quarter over quarter. This proves the segment is growing at a sustained pace.

In terms of margins, Amazon’s gross profit rose 16% to $103.4 billion, with gross margin expanding 1.3 percentage points to 48.47% compared to the same period last year. Operating income also rose 18% to $25 billion, while net income rose 6% to $21.2 billion, or $1.98 per share (basic).

To me, what’s pretty notable is the gross margin expansion, especially since Amazon has historically been a low-margin, high-volume business. That 1.3% gross margin improvement tells me the revenue mix is changing. More money is coming from higher-margin products like advertising, AWS, and subscription services, which can offset the much lower margins from shipping Amazon packages to doorsteps.

For the full year, Amazon’s revenue rose 12% to $716.9 billion. Gross profit rose 16% to $360.5 billion, and gross margin expanded 1.44 percentage points to cross the halfway mark at 50.29%. Again, I think that margin expansion is worth considering; only a few years ago, this would have seemed like a dream. And finally, operating income rose 17% to $80 billion, while net income increased 31% to $77.7 billion, or $7.29 a share (basic).

With the financials covered, let’s have a look at what’s happening with the stock itself and whether the current levels make sense for long-term investors.

AMZN stock profile and valuation

At the time of publication, Amazon shares trade at around $205. The stock’s down around 10% over the past 6 months and 11% year to date. Zoom out to a 5-year scale, and Amazon is up roughly 26%. That’s a pretty modest return for a company of this caliber, especially compared to its peers in the MAG7.

So what’s behind the relatively sluggish momentum? It’s the sector-wide sell-off punishing heavy CapEx guidance. Amazon continues to invest heavily in AI infrastructure and data center buildout; management expects $200 billion in CapEx for 2026, the highest among the Magnificent 7 to date.

However, many think that Amazon is stuck in a narrative vacuum. What worries investors is that AWS is still growing at a solid rate and has historically been Amazon’s growth star, but the growth is no longer as explosive as it used to be to get investors excited.

Meanwhile, Amazon’s e-commerce arm still carries the stigma of being a low-margin business, and the higher-growth drivers like advertising and AI services haven’t yet reached the scale where experts consider them a significant factor in forward-looking models.

In other words, the company is a bit too diversified for any single narrative to dominate, and at this point, we’re a little too early in the next chapter for the market to fully price it in. However, I think this presents a window of opportunity for long-term investors willing (and able) to be patient.

On the valuation front, Amazon’s forward P/E ratio (GAAP) is 26x, about 42% above the sector median. This is how much investors are paying today for each dollar of future earnings.

Is this expensive? Some might say yes, but my position is that 26x doesn’t fully capture the potential compounding value of Amazon’s businesses. Through the stock, you’d get exposure to e-commerce and logistics. At the same time, you get a piece of one of the most dominant cloud providers in the AI era.

For investors with a 3- to 5-year view, this is a fair entry point. Surely not a bargain despite depressed levels, but certainly it could be a good opportunity to start a position, given the quality of what you’re buying into.

So, the numbers are great, and the valuation seems fair. That leaves us with figuring out where Amazon is heading from here. And to get answers to that question, let’s look at the company’s most promising growth drivers.

Potential driver #1: Alexa+ launched to all U.S. users

Amazon is upgrading Alexa through Alexa+, the next-gen assistant that has been capitalizing on AI over the years. The product’s been in early access since last year and launched fully for all US users on Feb. 4, 2026.

Alexa+ is free for Prime subscribers and priced at $19.99 per month for non-Prime users. I think bundling Alexa+ with Prime is a smart move, as it gives more value to Prime and will likely help with subscriber retention.

Now, the early numbers have been encouraging. To quote the company, Alexa+ is seeing “twice the conversations, three times the purchases, five times the recipe requests.” Incorporating LLMs into the mix seemed to make conversations with Alexa+ feel more “natural,” which, in turn, can increase user engagement. This is an excellent step forward and adds further value and helps justify the price tag.

Plus, according to Amazon, there are over 600 million Alexa-enabled devices worldwide. If Amazon could convert 5% of these users at $19.99 a month, Amazon is looking at potentially $7.2 billion in additional annual revenue. At 10%? $14.4 billion annually.

To sweeten the deal, that potential revenue would come from a division that has historically been one of Amazon’s slowest-growing businesses. So, if the move pans out, I’d be watching out for an Alexa+ subscriber count appearing in the company’s future financials.

Potential Driver #2: The ad business is quietly becoming the second AWS

The other thing I liked about Amazon’s financials was the ad numbers. In the fourth quarter, Amazon’s ad business revenue rose 23% to $21.3 billion. In 2025, ad revenue reached $68 billion, and the company’s already making moves to further add value to its advertisers.

Recently, Amazon announced deals with Netflix (NFLX) and Spotify (SPOT) that allow it to offer programmatic ad spots on both platforms through its own Amazon’s Demand-Side Platform (DSP). Both Netflix and Spotify boast some of the largest user bases in the world, so this deal makes DSP a better choice for advertisers seeking broader reach without having to manage multiple ad platforms. Plus, the service comes with Amazon’s own first-party data.

Amazon also released AI-powered ad and creative agent tools in September 2025, enabling brands to build full-funnel campaigns that span from awareness to purchase. That, along with the Netflix/Spotify deals, adds more value for advertisers. If it works, more ad spend will remain within Amazon’s ecosystem, building brand loyalty and strengthening its moat against competitors.

Personally, I think this ad business is the most underappreciated part of Amazon, and the market still treats it as an e-commerce add-on rather than a standalone revenue stream. But I don’t think that will last.

But of course, investments aren’t without risks, and there’s one situation in Europe that I believe warrants investors' attention.

Potential risk: Germany just banned Amazon’s algorithmic price controls

On the same day as the earnings release (Feb 5, 2026), Germany’s Federal Cartel Office, or Bundeskartellamt, handed down a ruling that prohibits Amazon from using algorithmic price controls in the German marketplace.

Now, compared with the company’s increased capital spending, this news might seem inconsequential. However, it carries implications for the business and may factor into Amazon’s future headwinds.

For those unfamiliar, Amazon had automated systems that flagged or removed third-party seller listings if the algorithm deemed the prices too high. The German regulator considers these rules non-transparent. According to them, sellers cannot predict when the algorithm will cut their visibility, which directly affects their competitiveness.

On top of the prohibition, the regulator ordered Amazon to pay €59 million (roughly $70 million) in disgorgement of profits, effectively requiring Amazon to return the profits earned through the practices. This is also the first time Germany has used its power to recover funds from a tech platform.

So why is that important? Well, Amazon controls about 60% of online retail sales in Germany and hosts thousands of 3rd-party sellers on Amazon.de.

If the EU adopts similar reasoning under the Digital Markets Act, these restrictions could extend across all member states. Remember, the ruling was coordinated with the European Commission, which raises a very real possibility.

Now, Amazon has one month to appeal to the Federal Court of Justice, nine months to comply, and three months to submit compliance solutions. I think the risk may be mitigated in the near term. However, the roughly €59 million order is explicitly labeled as a “partial” amount, so more could follow. Certainly, the financial hit from this single ruling is a drop in the bucket for Amazon, but the precedent it sets is not.

What’s my verdict on Amazon?

I think the market is missing one thing about Amazon: the quality of its revenue is changing quickly. Five years ago, its earnings were largely driven by low-margin retail. Today, AWS accounts for most of Amazon’s profitability.

But with advertising contributing nearly $69 billion annually (and growing at 20+%), this is by far what I’m most excited about. And let’s not forget Prime Video is also transforming into a legitimate advertising platform through its content. Each of these businesses offers a meaningful growth potential for Amazon, and considering the company’s reach and balance sheets, the compounding potential is promising.

Of course, we can’t discount the recent regulatory risk, but I don’t think long-term investors should worry. Amazon’s balance sheet provides a moat to withstand that kind of uncertainty. Moreover, I believe the recent price weakness represents a discounted entry point for investors with a three- to five-year investing horizon. With that, and everything I mentioned above, my Strong Buy rating remains firmly in place.

data.title: Essent Group: Employment Threats Still A Concern
data.description:
Essent Group Ltd. (ESNT) is driven by issues in the employment market, which we mentioned in our previous coverage. The issue of employment is a central market theme right now because it relates to the proliferation of AI and also influences other factors important to Essent's business, like rates, concerning their fixed income reserve portfolio, and obviously claims, as well as demand for mortgages and housing in general. Reserving has risen because of the fragility in employment, and while it appears under control for the moment, hence the pause on rate cuts, we still have concerns in general around job growth and want to stay on the sidelines with this until we are more confident about the tenor in AI and agentic AI development, which has become too important of a trading factor in US markets - one that Essent has not seemed to be affected by yet as maybe it should.

Results

They are seeing solid growth in general. Housing starts are picking up, and so are mortgage originations, which will be the most direct KPI for a private mortgage insurer like Essent. These have started to steadily pick up since 2023, although considerably lower than the low-interest COVID-19 interest rate period. More in line with pre-COVID levels.

Consequently, premiums earned and policies written are growing. Note that net investment income is also growing. Reserving is also growing, related to macro, which we will comment on.

Looking at the portfolio in fixed income for a moment, we can make some comments about how macro might influence the performance of their available-for-sale assets. Fixed maturity and short-term investments are both classified as available for sale and reported at fair value as of the balance sheet date. In other words, it would seem available for sale is how they classify everything that they are holding in their reserve portfolio, taking a look at the balance sheet and seeing that all their investments and investments available for sale and reported at market value coincide, not much at book value, it would seem. We also say right away that they don't seem to have almost any equity exposure in their available-for-sale portfolio.

They have quite a lot of longer-term assets with more than 5 years in effective duration, meaning around 7+ years in average time to maturity. In terms of the yield curve, around 7 years and beyond is where changes in YTM, as per the new rate-cutting regime of the Fed, start to have a decreasingly profound effect. Already from 7 to 10 years, the decline in YTMs over the last 12 months goes from 60 to 44 bps, where between 6 months and 7 years, the decline in YTMs is around 60 to a peak of 80 bps at the 2-year mark. After 7 years, the impact of longer-term and structural considerations around rates starts to matter more, while before 7 years is the sweet spot of duration sensitivity and leverage to rate cuts, which we believe have a decent chance of continuing despite the apparent pause by the Fed.

The argument for more cuts coming from the Fed, despite some improvements in unemployment rates that motivated the current pause on cuts, is the renewed job cut surge in January. Moreover, the low-hire and low-fire environment presents an issue both for employment and even just through employment anxiety on consumer confidence. These are downside risks to YTM, which is good for the bond portfolio. On the other hand, the higher reserves for insurance claims by Essent over the last couple of years would be motivated by the increase in unemployment rates as well. While some bps in cuts from here on out with a couple of years of duration sensitivity, maybe a 1 pct cut to rates in the medium term, might lift investment income by $100 million (around 60% exposure to medium-term rates, 1 pct point YTM declines and around 3 years in duration so 60%*1%*3 years*$6 billion FI investments = approx $100 million), it could easily be offset by further increases in claims associated with risks to the growth mandate as well, with provisions doubling since 2024. Moreover, the 40% exposed to longer-term rates could be hit by higher, not lower, YTMs associated with a weakening status of the USD as a reserve currency, where the debasement trade has been dictating the long side of the yield curve. This could dampen the investment gains.

Bottom Line

It's an issue of direction. Threats to the growth mandate are going to matter for a business that depends on the credit of households to service mortgages. There are threats to that, especially when structural changes to employment from AI can affect employment and an independent debasement trade affecting markets, and the considerable exposure of ESNT to the long end of the yield curve. Even if unemployment stays at the levels at the opening of this year and doesn't rise, which we think is possible given the sustained low-hire and fire environment, there is still room for provisions to climb a little. It's not a no-brainer bet, and in this market, that means we want to stay away.
